payload.text:
I want an instructional design assistant app that creates a draft course design map. Create an app that does the job outlined in this prompt that a user would put into a chatbot:

ROLE
You are an instructional designer and evaluation expert.

TASK
Create a draft course design map.

PROCESS
Analyze this attached design document which shows the content of [an undergraduate Math] course. This design document contains module objectives under section headers titled Module Introduction. I have pasted the course learning objectives below. Use these module objectives and course learning objectives to build a course design map.

Follow these steps:
1) Review the Course Learning Objectives (CLOs) listed below.
2) For each CLO, identify every module objective in the design document that aligns with it.
3) Under each module objective, identify and list all related course items including [Add course-specific items like Readings & Multimedia pages, quizzes, assignments, discussions].
4) Exclude all content from the Instructor Resources, Student Resources, and Getting Started modules.

FINAL OUTPUT
Your final output should present each CLO and its aligned module objectives, with the relevant course items nested beneath each module objective.

Each CLO section should also contain a “Findings” section that expresses howe well each CLO is addressed in the course, and recommendations for improvement.


COURSE LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs):
[list CLOs here]

**Mapping Course Design**

I'm working on an Instructional Design Assistant. The goal is to build a "Course Design Map" generator. I'm focusing on how to translate Course Learning Objectives (CLOs) and Module Objectives (MOs) into a visual and functional map. Right now I'm thinking about the structure and key elements needed for effective course design. I'm taking the expertise in course design, and creating a map of the course for designers.


**Refining User Input**

I've been focusing on the user input stage for the Instructional Design Assistant. Now, I'm thinking about the user experience with CLO and design document entry. I'm supporting both text pasting and file uploads for the design document. I'm also adding a "Course Type" selection for tailored results. The "Generate Design Map" button is key, and I'm designing a structured map display. I'm aiming for a clean, modern aesthetic.


**Building the User Interface**

I'm now diving into the implementation phase. I'm focusing on the component structure: `Layout`, `CLOInput`, `DocumentUpload`, `ResultDisplay`, `Header`, and `LoadingOverlay`. I'm starting by building out the key components. The tech stack includes `index.html`, `index.tsx`, `App.tsx`, and all the components. I've also planned the prompt logic within `services/gemini.ts`, and I'm designing the input process, including text pasting and file uploads, and a course type selector for more tailored outputs.

entries.description: App metadata and permissions
Main HTML entry point
React entry point
Shared type definitions
Service for interacting with the Gemini API
A visually appealing loading overlay
Displays the generated design map
Main application logic

payload.text:
In the form, create optional fields for Program Learning Objectives (PLOs) and University Learning Objectives (ULOs) too.

Also, create another field that says, "Exclude content from the following:" and have suggested text in grey that reads "Instructor Resources, Student Resources, and Getting Started modules. Also exclude any unpublished course content."

**Considering Optional Fields**

I'm currently focused on incorporating optional fields into the instructional design assistant app. Specifically, I'm thinking about including "Program Learning Objectives (PLOs)" and "University Learning Objectives (ULOs)" as optional inputs for users. This will provide more flexibility.

entries.description: Update the service to handle PLOs, ULOs, and custom exclusions in the alignment mapping logic.
Add fields for PLOs, ULOs, and Exclusions with improved UI layout.

payload.text: In the form, put PLOs and ULOs in their own sections (3 & 4 respectively). Also, could this app handle uploads in docx or PDF format?

**Refining the Interface**

I'm working on the Instructional Design Assistant app, and my current focus is a much-needed overhaul of the form layout. Specifically, I'm reorganizing the form to prioritize Program and University Learning Objectives. This should enhance user experience by streamlining data entry and improving the natural workflow. I'm aiming for a more intuitive and visually clear design.


**Expanding File Support**

I've determined the need to support `.docx` and `.pdf` uploads in the Instructional Design Assistant app. I'm leaning toward using `mammoth.js` for `.docx` extraction and `pdfjs-dist` for `.pdf`, leveraging either script tags or dynamic imports for implementation. The goal is cleaner file handling and streamlined content ingestion for the user. I'm also refining the form structure, with PLOs and ULOs in dedicated numbered sections.


**Maintaining the Clean Design**

I'm now prioritizing visual consistency with the existing clean, professional style in the Instructional Design Assistant app. Form layout remains a key area, and I'm updating my design specification to reflect section numbering and improved input sequencing. The aim is a logical flow. I'm also confirming the CDN links for `mammoth.js` and `pdf.js` for file parsing, with `pdf.js` using a worker to handle PDF text extraction correctly. I'm keeping the sleek design as is.

entries.description: Added mammoth.js for DOCX parsing and pdf.js for PDF parsing support.
Reorganized form sections for PLOs and ULOs. Implemented DOCX and PDF text extraction functionality.

For section 5, don't just say "Exclusions" but include the full phrase "Exclude content from the following:"

I've been working on refining the UI labels for the "ID Design Mapper" app. Recently, I've updated sections 3 and 4, replacing the "Optional:" placeholder with an "[Optional]" suffix on the section labels themselves, as requested. This should provide better clarity. Next up is label updates.

**Analyzing Section Placement**

I've been re-evaluating the component's layout, specifically how to integrate the new section asking "Where are module objectives located?". Currently, there's a left column with sections for Course Context, CLOs, PLOs, ULOs, and Exclusions, and a right column for the design document content. I am thinking of adding this new section into the left column before the design document content. I will consider the best section to add it before to ensure optimal design flow.


**Adding Objective Location Section**

I'm now incorporating a new section focused on module objective locations. I'm adding it as a new numbered section on the left column, before the design document content. This section will include a textarea with starter text, and I need to update `App.tsx` and the relevant services to pass this new information to the Gemini service. Specifically, I'm setting up state for `objectiveLocation` and creating the corresponding UI.


**Integrating New Parameter**

I'm updating `geminiService.ts` to include the `objectiveLocation` parameter in the system instruction. I've examined `App.tsx` and realized that inserting the new section before the Design Document Content makes sense as it will now be the last section. The starter text for the objective location is determined by a prompt requirement. Now, I will update the `services/geminiService.ts` to accept the new `objectiveLocation` and include it in the prompt.

payload.text: For "Course Context" use a dropdown menu that contains the following options: undergraduate, master's, doctoral, professional development, Other" If the user selects "Other" show a text box that allows users to type inside of.

**Considering UI Improvements**

I'm currently focused on improving the user interface for the "Course Context" input. The goal is to replace the text input field with a dropdown menu offering predefined options: Undergraduate, Master's, Doctoral, Professional Development, and Other. The most recent consideration is implementing a text box dynamically appearing if "Other" is selected, allowing custom input.


**Refining State Management**

I'm now refining the state management for the course context selection. Initially, I thought about managing the dropdown's selected option and a separate custom text input value. But, I've realized I can streamline it. I'm going to use one state variable for the UI's selected context (the dropdown's selection), and another that holds the "actual" context for API calls, which can be dynamically updated based on the UI selection (dropdown option or textbox value). This will significantly simplify the logic and improve readability.

payload.text:
Change the name of the app from "Curriculum Alignment Simplified" to "Course Alignment Assistant"

Also, remove the subtitle.

Also, add an accordion section titled "Alignment Needs To Be Verified" that contains the following text:

"Course design maps are usually the result of collaboration between an instructional design consultant and a faculty developer. This process is not meant to replace that; instead it should serve as a first draft or a next-best option.

After getting the draft design map, carefully check it yourself against the course content, use your best judgement to make edits, and then verify the alignment with the Faculty Developer (FD), Instructor of Record (IoR), or whomever has the qualifications or authority to approve the accuracy of the draft map.

Treat the AI-generated map as a draft, not a final product."

**Refining The Focus**

I've decided to rename the app's initial phrase from "Curriculum Alignment Simplified" to "Course Alignment Assistant." Additionally, I'm removing the subtitle from the hero section to streamline the presentation. I'm also planning to incorporate a new accordion section, which will be titled "Alignment."


**Implementing The UI**

I'm now focusing on the UI changes. I'm updating `App.tsx` to reflect the name change, removing the subtitle. I'm also adding a new accordion section, prominently displayed, to emphasize the need for human verification in course alignment. I will use Tailwind classes for styling. I've got the state variable in place for the accordion's open/close state.


**Updating The Layout**

I've removed the subtitle from the header to streamline it, adjusting the margin accordingly. I'm also adding an accordion section after the header. This section will contain an accordion to introduce the new section. The accordion is designed to provide further information to the user on how the AI driven assistant aids in course alignment. I am using Tailwind classes.


**Adding Accordion Details**

I'm now refining the accordion's content. I've built the button and it's responsive to user interactions, opening and closing smoothly. I have implemented a component that provides more detail for the verification information. The styling is also complete. I'm focusing on ensuring it clearly conveys the importance of human oversight.


**Clarifying The Guidance**

I'm working on the content for the accordion details, focusing on clear guidance. I've written text explaining that the AI generates a first draft, not a final product. I've also incorporated the need for human verification and edits by relevant personnel. The goal is to stress that the AI-generated map requires careful review and approval.

entries.description: Changed app title, removed hero subtitle, and added a mandatory verification disclaimer accordion section.

payload.text: Make the header background the same color blue that you used in the app title previously. For the accordion text, change the color to a yellow that goes well with the blue.

Okay, I'm focusing on modifying `App.tsx` now. I'm zeroing in on the header's background color. The goal is to use the same blue that "Simplified" used to have. The target color I am seeking to reuse from past configurations, is `text-blue-400`.


**Matching the Blue Theme**

I found the original title used `text-blue-400`. The *primary* blue on buttons seems to be `bg-blue-600`. I'll stick with `bg-blue-600` for now in the header, since that's the current plan. I'll test text-blue-400 as a fallback.


**Refining Color Choices**

I'm now zeroing in on the specific color changes, and refining my previous conclusions. The header will be `bg-blue-600`, consistent with the buttons. For the accordion, I'm going with `text-yellow-500` for the title and icon, and I'll consider a more neutral background like `bg-slate-50` or similar for the accordion.


**Revisiting Blue Details**

I'm revisiting the blue theme, specifically the shade used in the title's "Simplified." I've confirmed `#60a5fa` for `text-blue-400`. For the header, I think `bg-blue-600` is safer than `bg-blue-500` for accessibility and a more professional look.

I've got a revised plan now. I'll change the header's background to `bg-blue-600`. Also, I'll switch the accordion button's text and icon to `text-yellow-500`. payload.text: Please move the Help Center accordion to the far left of the banner, not under the title.

**Analyzing Layout Options**

Okay, I'm now focusing on moving the "Help Center" accordion. I am exploring the `App.tsx` file and see the banner's container (`header`) has constraints. The title is centered. I'm visualizing how to shift the accordion to the left, separate from the title. I need to figure out how to best position it.


**Refining Positioning Strategy**

I'm now zeroing in on positioning, and am considering either absolute positioning or a flex row. I'm leaning toward making the banner container `relative` and positioning the Help Center `absolute` bottom-0 left-0. Alternatively, a grid layout could also work, or even better, a flex row. I've noted that `flex flex-col` is causing stacking. My goal is to achieve the user's "far left" requirement.


**Optimizing Container Placement**

I've got a clearer vision now. The `max-w-7xl mx-auto` container provides a simple solution: place a `relative` div inside it. The previous attempt at `flex-col` caused unwanted stacking. The goal is to move the Help Center outside the `text-center` div. I can absolutely position it relative to the header, or just inside the 7xl container, which should not push the title down significantly. I'm focusing on the `pt-10 pb-6` now.


**Evaluating Placement Refinement**

Okay, so I've decided to prioritize the "far left of the banner" requirement, which feels like a corner placement to me. I've chosen to use `absolute` positioning for the Help Center, and I'm currently experimenting with `bottom-4` and `left-4`. The header is set to `relative`. The banner height is important, and I am avoiding a "huge" header. My goal is to ensure the Help Center either overlaps the main content slightly, or is just right on the edge of the banner.


**Exploring Layout Refinements**

I'm now considering the 'far left of the *banner*' requirement, not just the content width. I've realized centering the title presents a challenge. A flex row seems best: I can try a `max-w-7xl mx-auto flex flex-col md:flex-row` setup for larger screens, ordering the elements to position the Help Center. It'll be a row layout: the Help Center, then the centered title, then a spacer. This would place the help center on the left, independent of the title's centering. I also am considering the visual impact of an accordion on the left - it may look better pinned to the bottom left.


**Finalizing Bottom-Left Placement**

Okay, I'm now fine-tuning the bottom-left placement. While absolute positioning within the `max-w-7xl` container seemed promising, the existing negative margins (`-mt-8`) near the "Alignment Needs To Be Verified" section cause a positioning conflict. I will try positioning the Help Center relative to the header. I'm focusing on ensuring it appears visually correct.

entries.description: Moved the Help Center accordion to the bottom-far-left of the banner using absolute positioning. This separates it from the title's vertical stack on desktop while maintaining a clean, centered title.

payload.text:
In the form, change "CLOS" to "CLOs" and "PLOS" to "PLOs" and "ULOS" to "ULOs"

Also, change the item in the Help Center from "Companion App: Export Tool" to "Companion App: The eCampus Content Export Tool"

Finally, change the name of this app from "eCampus Course Alignment Assistant" to "The eCampus Course Alignment Assistant"

**Refining Text Updates**

Okay, I'm now focused on the text modification task for the app. The user highlighted the changes in form labels, specifically replacing acronyms. I'm taking note of the specified transformation: "CLOS" to "CLOs", "PLOS" to "PLOs". The next acronym, "ULOS", requires further clarification from the user, so I have a question waiting on the next acronym.


**Investigating Variable Names**

I'm now diving deeper into the meaning behind the user's request. It seems the acronyms are already correctly rendered in the interface text. I'm focusing on the state names and placeholder text. I've noted the existing variable names: `clos`, `plos`, and `ulos`. I'm starting to think the concern may be with the placeholder text for the input fields, as I saw there were different placeholders for the different labels. My next step will be analyzing these.


**Clarifying Acronym Placement**

I'm now zeroing in on the exact location of the acronym changes. The user's concern seems to extend beyond just form labels, as I've confirmed those in `App.tsx` are already correct. I'm expanding my review to include the text within the `MappingResult` component and the `geminiService.ts` file, where I see the headers are as follows: `COURSE LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)`, `PROGRAM LEARNING`. It seems like those may need modification as well. I need to make sure I am looking at all of the potential changes in the right places!


**Updating Textual References**

Okay, I've got a clearer picture now. I will ensure all labels and placeholders consistently use the mixed-case plural acronym forms (CLOs, PLOs, ULOs). I'm focusing on the `App.tsx` file to update the header title and help center link text. I am double-checking that the placeholders are consistent and correct as well. payload.text: Is it possible to increase the size of the text?

**Analyzing Text Size Options**

I'm currently focused on how to best increase text size within the React application. I've noted it's built with React and Tailwind CSS. The hero section, accordions, and large form are key areas to consider for adjustments. I'm exploring the best approach: globally defined classes, or component-specific overrides, or something else.


**Expanding Text Size Globally**

I'm now prioritizing the application-wide text size adjustments. My aim is a systematic increase. For inputs and textareas, currently `text-sm`, I'll consider `text-base` or `text-lg`. I'll also modify labels from `text-xs` to `text-sm`, and will look at headings, findings, and recommendations. I'll increase sizes in a stepwise way using the size map provided, for a holistic approach to readability.

I will update the application to increase the text size across all major components—including form labels, inputs, textareas, result mappings, and the loading screen—to enhance readability and provide a more accessible user experience.

### Specification of Updates

1.  **Global Base Scaling**:
    *   Form labels will move from `text-xs` (12px) to `text-sm` (14px).
    *   Body text in accordions and results will move from `text-sm` (14px) to `text-base` (16px) or `text-lg` (18px).
    *   Large input fields and textareas will move from `text-sm` to `text-base` or `text-lg`.
2.  **Form Enhancements**:
    *   All textareas (CLOs, PLOs, etc.) will use `text-lg` for easier drafting.
    *   The "Design Document Content" area (Field 8) will be upgraded to `text-base` to handle large amounts of text comfortably.
3.  **Result Page Improvements**:
    *   Findings and Recommendations will be upgraded to `text-lg` and `text-base` respectively.
    *   The Executive Summary will use `text-xl` for higher prominence.
4.  **Loading UI**:
    *   The "Analyzing" message will be increased to `text-2xl`.

payload.text:

**Integrating QM Standards**

I'm now integrating the QM+ standards (2.1-2.5) into the report. The system instructions and prompt in `geminiService.ts` require updates, specifically incorporating the new evaluation criteria. I'm also revising `MappingResult.tsx` to align the UI and export logic with the new report template's sections: Executive Summary, QM Feedback (2.1-2.5), CLO alignment, and Module alignment. Finally, I'll update the relevant types in `types.ts` to accommodate the QM feedback fields. The AI's behavior will be to evaluate specifically against QM 2.1 through 2.5.


**Structuring Report Elements**

I'm now structuring the report. The output will have dedicated QM standards sections and a Word export mirroring the template images. I'm updating the instruction text and adding a new "Feedback on Objectives" section on the results page. This "Feedback" section will display distinct boxes for QM 2.1 through 2.5. The layout will maintain the brand.

payload.text: Display the character limit and count at the bottom of the Design Document Content window

**Adding Character Count Feature**

I need to determine the best method for implementing this, probably using state within the component to manage the character count.


**Defining Character Limit**

Okay, I've located the target `textarea` in `App.tsx` and am now solidifying the character limit strategy. After consideration, I'm setting a 100,000-character limit, as this strikes a balance between user experience and system constraints. This will be the upper bound of the counter. I'll make this a constant and incorporate it into the UI.


**Positioning Counter Implementation**

I've decided to place the character counter directly below the existing `textarea` element within its column. The plan is to append a `div` element that displays the current character count against the 100,000-character limit. It'll show `Character Count: {documentContent.length} / 100,000`. My prior design decisions have cemented my design strategy.
